mnemonics.title: SCOPE Uses
mnemonics.content: Remember SCOPE - Static members, Class definitions, Outside definitions, Parent/Base class, Enum class, Everything global - covering all uses of :: operator.

mnemonics.title: Two Dots Rule
mnemonics.content: Use '.' when you have an object (obj.member), use '::' when you have a scope name (class::member, namespace::member). Think: 'Dots for Objects, Colons for Classes/Namespaces'.

mnemonics.title: Global First Aid
mnemonics.content: When local and global variables fight, use :: (first aid) to access the global one. The leading :: is like calling for help from global namespace.

acronyms.usage: Used to specify which scope an identifier belongs to - class, namespace, or global.
Prefix to access standard library components from the std namespace.
Using BaseClass::member to access base class members in inheritance hierarchy.

visualTips.visualization: Draw two boxes: Global box (outside) and Local box (inside function). Use :: to jump from Local to Global box to access variables.
Think of class as a blueprint (:: on outside) and object as the built item (. on inside). :: connects the outside definition to the class.
Draw std as a container box. Use std:: to reach inside the container. Without ::, you're outside looking in.

analogies.analogy: Like specifying room number in a building: :: is like saying 'Room 101' (global scope) to find someone when there are multiple 'John's in different rooms.
:: is like a name tag that says 'This function belongs to this class' - it's the return address on a package.
Enum class values are like items in a locked display case - you need the key (::) to access them: Color::Red, not just Red.
